Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study whenever I remembered or when something was due soon. With four online courses going at once, I just kind of bounced between whatever seemed most urgent. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything, then try to cram a bunch in at once. It wasn't organized at all, more like survival mode.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go through phases where I wouldn't study for a week, then panic when I realized deadlines were coming up. Other stuff in my life always seemed to take priority—work, family stuff, just being tired. I never really settled into a consistent weekly schedule, it was always reactive.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email notification about something due, or I'd suddenly remember I hadn't checked in for a while and feel guilty. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pop up and I'd think 'okay, I should at least do something today.' It was rarely because I was genuinely excited to learn, more like obligation or panic.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. I'd have brief stretches where I'd log in a few days in a row, but then life would get in the way and I'd fall off again. Without reliable internet at home, I had to plan around when I could get to the library or use someone else's connection, which made routine impossible.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and they helped concepts click better than just reading. I watched quite a bit when I could access them. The gamified tasks were also kind of helpful because they made things feel less overwhelming and gave me small wins. At least I felt like I was accomplishing something.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion boards most of the time. I posted a few things but it felt awkward and I never knew what to say. The readings I'd often skip or skim because they were so long and I couldn't focus. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment, I probably didn't do it.
Q7: If it was going to help me pass an assignment or if it felt quick and manageable, I'd do it. The gamified stuff worked on me because it felt achievable and gave immediate feedback. Anything that seemed like it would take forever or was too abstract, I'd put off. I needed to see the point right away.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—I barely had energy for the mandatory things. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself. Optional felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when I was already struggling to keep up.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs or save videos when I had internet access, then study them later at home without being online. I also used my phone to look up explanations on YouTube when the course materials confused me. A lot of my actual studying happened with notebooks and printed stuff, not on the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through my printed notes or rewatching downloaded videos, but none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend sometimes, quizzing each other verbally. The LMS only saw me when I was actively clicking around online, which wasn't that often given my situation.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once to download it, then that was the last time I'd click on it in the system. But I'd actually review those materials multiple times offline. My LMS activity probably looked way lower than my actual engagement with the content itself.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. I was stressed about money, working extra shifts, and I just felt completely overwhelmed. The course material started getting harder and I didn't have the foundation because I'd missed so many classes. I kind of shut down and avoided everything for a while.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with family drama, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. Confusion made it worse—if I didn't understand something, I'd just close the tab instead of pushing through. Competing responsibilities always won because they had more immediate consequences.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through stuff without really absorbing anything. I'd watch a video but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I was physically present in the LMS but mentally checked out. Those sessions felt pointless but I did them anyway to feel like I was trying.
Q15: I avoided the exam prep materials for way too long because they stressed me out. I knew they were important but looking at them made me anxious about how much I didn't know. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find another excuse. It was self-sabotage, honestly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd cram everything in the last day or two before something was due. My LMS activity probably spiked right before deadlines and was dead otherwise. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could make myself act.
Q17: When I got feedback it was usually too late to really change anything. By the time I saw comments on one assignment, I'd already submitted the next one making the same mistakes. It was discouraging more than helpful. I stopped checking feedback after a while because it just made me feel worse about myself.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with four online courses running simultaneously. I couldn't keep track of what was due when across all of them. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller pieces, which I appreciated, but overall the structure assumed I had more time and focus than I actually did.
Q19: The lack of flexibility in deadlines made things harder. If I missed something, I felt like I was already failing and that made me want to give up. The gamified tasks were easier to engage with because they felt low-stakes and fun. But the big exams and assignments felt impossible when I was already behind.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying but they're lost or confused. They might be clicking around looking for help or trying to understand what they're supposed to do, but never actually figure it out. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff that makes it hard to follow through even when they're putting in effort.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way—my LMS activity was low but I completed most assignments. I did a lot of work offline that didn't get tracked. Some people just learn differently or have circumstances that keep them away from the online platform but they're still engaged with the actual material.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on stuff and how long you were there, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or cared. They miss all the offline work, the mental struggle, the life circumstances. They're like looking at someone's footprints without knowing where they were actually trying to go.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just going through the motions but wanting to understand. For me, I rarely felt truly engaged this semester—I was mostly just trying to survive and get through it without completely failing.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how hard things were for me outside of school, or how much I actually wanted to do better but couldn't. It wouldn't show the anxiety I felt every time I logged in, or the studying I did offline. It definitely wouldn't capture that I was surprised I even made it through most of the assignments given everything going on.
